text: Neural machine translation (NMT) has recently emerged as a compelling alternative to traditional statistical machine translation by utilizing deep neural networks to map source sequences to target sequences. Most NMT architectures employ an encoder-decoder framework, where an encoder compresses a variable-length input sentence into a fixed-size vector representation from which a decoder generates the translation. In this study, we investigate the empirical properties of NMT systems by comparing the established RNN Encoder-Decoder with a novel architecture featuring a gated recursive convolutional neural network (grConv) encoder. Through a series of experiments on English-to-French translation, we demonstrate that while these models excel at translating short sentences with familiar vocabularies, their performance declines sharply as sentence length increases or when they encounter out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, our qualitative analysis reveals that the proposed grConv model is capable of capturing internal linguistic hierarchies, automatically learning a form of syntactic structure without explicit grammatical supervision. Our findings highlight current limitations in the representational capacity of fixed-length embeddings and suggest critical directions for improving the robustness of neural translation models.
